slides.title: Linux Hardening Masterclass
slides.subtitle:
Production Server Security & Defense-in-Depth
From Kernel Optimization to Access Control

list.text: Topic 1: Linux Hardening & The OS Attack Surface
Topic 2: Access Control: SSH & PAM Configuration
Topic 3: Kernel Parameter Tuning & Boot Security
Topic 4: Firewalls & Network Filters (nftables)
Topic 5: Filesystem Mounts & UGO/ACL Permissions
Topic 6: Mandatory Access Control (SELinux/AppArmor)
Topic 7: Auditing & Monitoring (auditd, Lynis, Fail2ban)
Topic 8: Sandbox Isolation (namespaces, cgroups, seccomp)
Topic 9: 10-Question Hardening Quiz

slides.title: Topic 1: Linux Security & Attack Surface
slides.subtitle: Understanding common threats, design philosophy, and least privilege

list.text: Cloud Footprint: Linux runs over 90% of public cloud instances; it is the primary target for attackers.
Kernel Complexity: The Linux kernel is massive, creating bugs and zero-day vulnerabilities (e.g., Dirty COW, PwnKit).
High-Value Targets: Database systems, web servers, and container clusters are high-priority ransomware targets.
Default Configuration Weaknesses: Standard distributions prioritize convenience over high-level security out of the box.
Banish Direct Root Logins: Disable root console access via network and enforce sudo usage.
Isolated Service Accounts: Run daemons (like nginx, postgres) under dedicated, non-privileged system accounts.
Disable Interactive Shells: Assign `/usr/sbin/nologin` or `/bin/false` to service user accounts.
Sudo Fine-Graining: Allow specific administrative commands rather than granting absolute `ALL` access.
Exposed Ports: Unused open listening sockets (e.g., unauthenticated Redis, backup SSH servers).
Credential Stuffing: Brute-force attacks against weak user passwords on port 22.
Local Privilege Escalation (LPE): Attackers exploit local bugs to transition from a low-privilege user to root.
Supply Chain Hacks: Malicious libraries injected into package registries (e.g., the XZ Utils backdoor).
Network Layer: Port filtering, IP whitelisting, and DDoS firewalls.
Identity Layer: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), public-key SSH, and PAM rules.
Kernel/OS Layer: LSM restrictions (SELinux/AppArmor), sysctl parameters, and boot protection.
File Layer: Strict read/write permissions, partition boundaries (nosuid), and integrity scans.

slides.title: Topic 2: Identity & Access Management
slides.subtitle: Hardening the secure shell daemon, configuring PAM modules, and managing sudo policies

list.text: PermitRootLogin no: Block direct logins to the root account.
PasswordAuthentication no: Force cryptographic SSH public-key verification.
ClientAliveInterval 300 / ClientAliveCountMax 2: Auto-disconnect idle sessions after 10 minutes.
AllowUsers / AllowGroups: Whitelist specific administrative accounts that are allowed to log in.

box.title: /etc/ssh/sshd_config
box.code:
# Enforce SSH Protocol 2 only
Protocol 2

# Authentication controls
PermitRootLogin no
MaxAuthTries 3
PubkeyAuthentication yes
PasswordAuthentication no
PermitEmptyPasswords no

# Crypto optimization (Disable weak algorithms)
KexAlgorithms curve25519-sha256,curve25519-sha256@libssh.org,diffie-hellman-group16-sha512
Ciphers chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com,aes256-gcm@openssh.com
MACs hmac-sha2-512-etm@openssh.com

# User restriction
AllowGroups sysops security
X11Forwarding no

list.text: Layout: Located in `/etc/pam.d/`. Modular configurations for login, sshd, and sudo.
Complexity Enforcement: Use `pam_pwquality` to enforce password length, character mixes, and dictionary checks.
Account Lockouts: Configure `pam_faillock` (replaces older `pam_tally2`) to lock accounts after repeated login failures.
Access Controls: Use `pam_access.so` to restrict logins based on hostnames, origin IPs, or groups.

box.title: /etc/pam.d/common-password snippet
box.code:
# Enforce strong passwords using pam_pwquality.so
password required pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=14 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 reject_username enforce_for_root

# Lockout users after 5 failed attempts within 15 minutes (in common-auth)
auth required pam_faillock.so preauth silent deny=5 unlock_time=900
auth [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail deny=5 unlock_time=900

list.text: Use /etc/sudoers.d/: Do not edit `/etc/sudoers` directly. Use specialized files via `visudo -f`.
Avoid NOPASSWD: Requiring a password re-entry on elevation blocks automated keylogger payloads.
Strict Command Whitelisting: Restrict groups to specific safe commands (e.g., restarting system service only).
Enforce Sudo Logging: Route all sudo access logs to secure centralized files.

slides.title: Topic 3: System & Kernel Hardening
slides.subtitle: GRUB boot protection, kernel parameter tuning via sysctl, and module management

list.text: GRUB Passwords: Enforce a password to edit boot parameters, blocking attackers from appending `init=/bin/sh`.
Disable External Booting: Lock down hardware BIOS/UEFI to block boot from USB, CD/DVD, or Network PXE.
UEFI Secure Boot: Enforce signature checks on the kernel and drivers, preventing rootkits from loading during boot.
Protect /boot Partition: Mount `/boot` as read-only (`ro`) in production unless installing updates.
TCP SYN Cookies: Protect against TCP SYN flood DoS attacks by enabling cookies.
Disable IP Forwarding: Ensure the system does not act as a router and forward untrusted packets.
Block Source Routing: Ignore source-routed packets, which can allow spoofing attacks.
ASLR Enrichment: Maximize Address Space Layout Randomization to prevent buffer overflow attacks.

box.title: /etc/sysctl.d/99-security.conf
box.code:
# Enable TCP SYN Flood Protection
net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1

# Ignore ICMP Redirects (Prevent MITM)
net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0

# Enable Reverse Path Filtering (Prevent IP Spoofing)
net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1
net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 1

# Restrict dmesg access to root only
kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1

# Enable ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization)
kernel.randomize_va_space = 2

list.text: The Hazard: Unused modules (e.g. firewire, uncommon filesystems) expose complex kernel code to local exploits.
Blacklisting Modules: Create configurations in `/etc/modprobe.d/` to restrict auto-loading.
USB Storage Blacklist: Disable USB storage modules on high-security servers to prevent physical data extraction.
Disable Network Protocols: Blacklist complex unused protocols like DCCP, SCTP, RDS, and TIPC.

box.title: /etc/modprobe.d/security-blacklist.conf
box.code:
# Blacklist unused network protocols
install dccp /bin/true
install sctp /bin/true
install rds /bin/true
install tipc /bin/true

# Disable USB storage devices
install usb-storage /bin/true

# Disable legacy filesystems
install cramfs /bin/true
install freevxfs /bin/true
install hfs /bin/true
install jffs2 /bin/true

slides.title: Topic 4: Network Security & Firewalls
slides.subtitle: Firewall layout, default-deny rulesets with nftables, and socket bindings

list.text: Legacy Iptables: Hard to manage, slow for complex rules, utilizes redundant table schemas.
Modern Nftables: Unified framework, faster VM-like execution, structured CLI syntax.
Wrappers (UFW/Firewalld): Convenient syntax for basic tasks, but lack deep enterprise pipeline features.
Performance Benefits: Nftables matches packets faster with lower CPU overhead under high load.
Policy Default Drop: Set input, forward, and output base chains to drop by default.
Stateful Tracking: Allow existing established and related connections, but block new ones.
Loopback Whitelist: Explicitly allow all traffic on the localhost loopback interface (`lo`).
Selective Port Holes: Open ports only for documented services (e.g., 22 for SSH, 443 for HTTPS).

box.title: /etc/nftables.conf

list.text: Wildcard Binding Hazard: Services binding to `0.0.0.0` listen on all interfaces, exposing database ports to the internet.
Localhost Binding: Force localized system components (Redis, PostgreSQL, memcached) to bind to `127.0.0.1`.
Verify Listening Ports: Use modern socket audit commands to audit network sockets.
Network Namespace Isolation: Run application instances inside separate namespaces to restrict interface access.

box.title: Identifying Exposed Interfaces
box.code:
# List all listening TCP and UDP sockets with process name and PIDs
ss -tulpn

# Audit open ports using netstat (Legacy)
netstat -tulpn

# Bind Redis to localhost interface only (/etc/redis/redis.conf)
bind 127.0.0.1 -::1

# Bind PostgreSQL to localhost only (/etc/postgresql/15/main/postgresql.conf)
listen_addresses = 'localhost'

slides.title: Topic 5: Filesystem Security & Access Controls
slides.subtitle: Standard UGO permissions, SUID/SGID hazards, ACLs, and mounting partitions securely

list.text: Octal Notation: Read (4), Write (2), Execute (1). `755` means rwxr-xr-x.
Umask Configuration: Establishes default permission flags on file creation. Enforce `027` (files: `640`, folders: `750`).
Directory Integrity: Restrict read/write to files by disabling write permission for others (`go-w`).
Sensitive System Files: `/etc/passwd` must be write-locked, and `/etc/shadow` must be read-restricted to root (`600`).
SetUID (SUID): Executes a binary with the permissions of the file owner (often root). A major vector for local root escalation.
SetGID (SGID): Executes files with group owner privileges or forces subdirectories to inherit parent group ownership.
Sticky Bit: Restricts deletion within a shared directory (like `/tmp`) to the file owner and root.
Auditing SUID Files: Regularly scan filesystems to locate and verify all binaries carrying SUID flags.

box.title: Terminal Commands for Permission Enforcement
box.code:
# Find all SUID binaries on the filesystem owned by root
find / -user root -perm -4000 -exec ls -ld {} \; 2>/dev/null

# Find all world-writable files
find / -type f -perm -0002 -exec ls -l {} \; 2>/dev/null

# Configure granular permissions for a user via Access Control Lists (ACLs)
setfacl -m u:john:r-x /opt/secured_directory/

# Verify ACLs applied to the path
getfacl /opt/secured_directory/

list.text: noexec: Block execution of any binary files compiled inside that specific partition.
nosuid: Disable SetUID execution bits on the files mounted inside the partition.
nodev: Block processing of character or block special devices on the partition.
Target Locations: Apply `noexec,nosuid,nodev` settings to volatile paths like `/tmp`, `/var/tmp`, and `/dev/shm`.

box.title: /etc/fstab snippet
box.code:
# Secure mounting configuration for user writable directories
/dev/vg01/tmp-vol   /tmp       ext4    defaults,nodev,nosuid,noexec   0 2
/dev/vg01/vartmp-vol /var/tmp   ext4    defaults,nodev,nosuid,noexec   0 2

# Secure shared memory mount
tmpfs                /dev/shm   tmpfs   defaults,nodev,nosuid,noexec   0 0

# Mount /boot partition as read-only by default
UUID=xxxx-xxxx       /boot      ext2    defaults,ro                    0 2

slides.title: Topic 6: Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
slides.subtitle: SELinux context labeling, AppArmor path-based profiles, and debugging denial logs

list.text: Discretionary Access Control (DAC): Owners decide access (via standard UGO). If a root-owned service is compromised, the attacker inherits full root capabilities.
Mandatory Access Control (MAC): System-wide security rules defined by security modules (SELinux, AppArmor). Even if root is compromised, MAC rules restrict process behavior.
LSM Core: Linux Security Modules (LSM) act as kernel hooks to check permissions prior to execution.
Type Enforcement: Every process, file, directory, and port is assigned a security context (e.g. `httpd_t`, `httpd_sys_content_t`).
Execution Modes: Enforcing (rules are blocked and logged), Permissive (violations are logged but allowed), Disabled (no MAC protection).
Context Restoration: Use `restorecon` to reset file contexts to default policy settings.
Common Policy: Targeted policy confines selected network daemons, leaving user processes in standard DAC modes.
Path-Based Identification: Associates security profiles directly to absolute paths of executable binaries.
Simpler Design: Preferred by Ubuntu/Debian; easier to read and create than SELinux.
Enforce vs. Complain Mode: Complain mode registers violations without blocking; Enforce mode enforces rules.
Profile Location: Stored in `/etc/apparmor.d/` as clear text config profiles.

box.title: SELinux & AppArmor Administration
box.code:
# SELinux: Check enforcement status
sestatus

# SELinux: Set execution mode to enforcing
sudo setenforce 1

# AppArmor: Check profile loaded status
sudo aa-status

# AppArmor: Switch a profile to enforce mode
sudo aa-enforce /usr/sbin/nginx

# Debugging MAC Denials (Search log files for audit errors)
sudo grep -i "denied" /var/log/audit/audit.log
sudo grep -i "apparmor" /var/log/syslog

slides.title: Topic 7: System Auditing & Intrusion Detection
slides.subtitle: Kernel level system call monitoring with auditd, Fail2ban brute-force protection, and Lynis security scans

list.text: Real-Time Tracking: Kernel-level audit daemon tracking file changes, authentication attempts, and command execution.
File Integrity Monitoring: Establish audit watches (`-w`) to detect modifications in critical system configuration paths (e.g. `/etc/passwd`).
System Call Audit: Audit syscall executions (like `execve` or `socket`) based on user IDs.
Analysis Tools: Use `ausearch` and `aureport` to parse binary audit logs.

box.title: /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules
box.code:
# Monitor modifications to the system user database
-w /etc/passwd -p wa -k user_modifications
-w /etc/shadow -p wa -k user_modifications
-w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k sudo_modifications

# Audit execution of system calls by non-privileged accounts
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k execution_audit

# Lock the configuration (make rules read-only until reboot)
-e 2

box.title: Automated IP Blocking via Log Analysis

list.text: Log Scanning: Continuously scans server log files (e.g. `/var/log/auth.log`) matching regex patterns of failures.
Dynamic Firewall Blocks: Triggers immediate firewall rules (iptables/nftables) to drop packets from source IPs exceeding threshold failures.
Alert Notifications: Can be configured to email security administrators immediately upon blocking an IP.
Custom Jails: Define distinct ban parameters for SSH, Nginx, database ports, and mail servers.

box.title: /etc/fail2ban/jail.local
box.code:
[DEFAULT]
bantime = 3600
findtime = 600
maxretry = 5
action = %(action_mw)s

[sshd]
enabled = true
port = ssh
logpath = %(sshd_log)s
backend = %(sshd_backend)s

[nginx-http-auth]
enabled = true
filter = nginx-http-auth
port = http,https
logpath = /var/log/nginx/error.log

list.text: Post-Installation Scan: Identifies security misconfigurations, vulnerable packages, and deviations from CIS benchmarks.
Hardening Index: Calculates a numeric score representing overall system security posture.
Actionable Suggestions: Generates warnings and suggestions on configuration files, PAM rules, and permissions.
Automation Mode: Supports headless, non-interactive execution inside cron schedules or CI/CD pipelines.

slides.title: Topic 8: Container & Process Isolation Security
slides.subtitle: Linux namespaces, resource constraints using cgroups, and system call filtering with seccomp

list.text: PID Namespace: Isolates process ID allocation. Processes in a container cannot see processes running on the host.
NET Namespace: Virtualizes network interfaces, routing tables, and socket bindings.
MNT Namespace: Limits disk mount visibility, providing containerized storage instances.
User Namespace: Maps local container IDs (e.g. root) to high-value non-privileged user IDs on the host.
Resource Control: Dictates maximum limits on CPU, memory, network bandwidth, and disk I/O rates.
DoS Mitigation: Limits container resource usage to prevent memory leaks from crashing the host.
OOM Killer Protection: Places containers inside memory limits so the Out-of-Memory killer terminates the container, not system hosts.
cgroups v2: Unified hierarchy design in modern kernels, simplifying multi-resource limits.
The Concept: Restricts the system calls a process can make. A breached daemon cannot execute unauthorized system calls.
Block Unsafe Calls: Turn off kernel interfaces (like `swapon`, `reboot`, `ptrace`) for normal user-space programs.
Docker default profile: Blocks over 40 unsafe system calls out of ~300, shielding the host kernel from guest exploits.

box.title: /etc/systemd/system/app.service snippet
box.code:
[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node /opt/app/index.js
User=nodeguest
Group=nodeguest

# Enforce Systemd sandboxing directives
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=yes
PrivateTmp=yes
PrivateDevices=yes
NoNewPrivileges=yes
CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE
SystemCallFilter=~@clock @cpu-emulation @debug @keyring @module @obsolete @raw-io @reboot @swap

slides.title: Topic 9: Linux Hardening Quiz
slides.subtitle: Test your knowledge on SSH, sysctl, SELinux, permissions, and auditing

slides.question: Q1: Why is configuring 'PermitRootLogin no' in sshd_config considered a critical security step for Linux servers?
options: It forces attackers to compromise a regular user account first, preventing direct root credential brute-forcing.
It automatically encrypts the root password using Argon2.
It speeds up SSH connection negotiations.
slides.explanation: Correct! Disabling direct root logins forces attackers to guess both a custom user ID and password, and preserves user trace logs through `sudo` audit trails.

slides.question: Q2: Which sysctl parameter configuration is used to protect a server from TCP SYN flood Denial-of-Service attacks?
slides.explanation: Correct! Enabling **tcp_syncookies** allows the kernel to handle incoming SYN queues by encoding sequence numbers in packet headers, avoiding memory exhaustion.

slides.question: Q3: An administrator edits a file's permission flags to '4755'. What does the prefix '4' denote?
options: The file is encrypted with AES-256.
The file has the sticky bit enabled.
The file has SetUID (SUID) enabled, executing with the owner's privileges.
The file can only be modified by group owners.
slides.explanation: Correct! The **4** prefix sets the **SetUID (SUID)** bit, making the binary run with the owner's privileges (e.g., root). These should be audited strictly to prevent privilege escalation.

slides.question: Q4: Which option in /etc/fstab prevents the execution of binary files on a mounted partition?
slides.explanation: Correct! The **noexec** mount option prevents files on that partition from being executed, limiting write-heavy paths (like `/tmp`) from running malicious payloads.

slides.question: Q5: How does Mandatory Access Control (MAC) differ from traditional Discretionary Access Control (DAC)?
options: MAC relies solely on user ownership permissions.
MAC applies global security rules at the kernel level that even the root user must obey.
MAC disables standard file systems, requiring database storage.
MAC allows users to edit security policies of other users' folders.
slides.explanation: Correct! Under **MAC** (SELinux/AppArmor), the kernel checks centralized policies. Even if a process running as root is hijacked, the MAC policy restricts it from accessing unauthorized files.

slides.question: Q6: Which SELinux mode logs security violations but allows the system to run without blocking operations?
slides.explanation: Correct! **Permissive** mode allows operations to proceed but logs warnings, making it perfect for debugging policies before locking down environments in **Enforcing** mode.

slides.question: Q7: What does the 'fail2ban' service do when it detects multiple authentication failures from a single IP address in log files?
options: It sends a notification email and shuts down the SSH service daemon.
It dynamically creates firewall rules to block traffic from that IP for a set duration.
It resets the user's password to a secure random string.
It logs the IP address into the `/etc/hosts` blacklist file.
slides.explanation: Correct! **Fail2ban** acts dynamically by adding temporary packet-drop rules in iptables/nftables to block brute-force attempts from specific IPs.

slides.question: Q8: Which tool checks the system's security configuration, filesystems, and packages against CIS benchmarks, providing a hardening index?
slides.explanation: Correct! **Lynis** is an open-source security auditing tool designed for Unix-based hosts to evaluate configurations and suggest optimization steps.

slides.question: Q9: Which Linux namespace isolates network interfaces, routing tables, and listening ports?
slides.explanation: Correct! The **NET Namespace** isolates network resources, letting containers maintain virtual interfaces and private port bindings without conflict.

slides.question: Q10: What is the primary function of system call filtering using 'seccomp' profiles?
options: To encrypt all system logs at the storage layer.
To restrict the system calls a process can make, reducing the kernel attack surface.
To configure network firewall access parameters.
slides.explanation: Correct! **Seccomp** restricts process system calls, limiting what an attacker can do if they exploit user-space bugs to run shellcode.

list.text: Identity: Disable password auth and root SSH logins. Configure Fail2ban.
Kernel: Set secure sysctl parameters. Lock down the GRUB bootloader.
Network: Enforce default-drop nftables firewall rules. Bind databases locally.
Filesystem: Audit SUID flags, set restrictive umasks, and mount `/tmp` as `noexec`.
MAC Protection: Keep SELinux or AppArmor in Enforcing mode.
Audit & Monitor: Configure auditd watches on critical files, and review suggestions from Lynis.

box.content:
🐧 Server security is a process, not a state.
Start applying defense-in-depth principles to your Linux nodes today!
box.note: Verify Configurations • Limit Permissions • Log Everything

footer.tagline: Empowering administrators to build secure infrastructure
